$comment: Project settings for Claude Code. Checked into git so the whole team shares
the same guardrails. Personal, machine-specific overrides go in
.claude/settings.local.json (gitignored).
permissions.allow lists the tools Claude may use without prompting.
permissions.deny is a belt-and-braces glob layer ON TOP OF the guard hooks,
which now ship in the spec-workflow plugin (not this file). The hooks do the
real work because they scan full command strings and resolve symlinks, which
glob rules cannot. See the plugin's hooks/README.md for the design and
self-tests; the hook wiring lives in the plugin's hooks/hooks.json.
spec-workflow.json is denied here AND in the guards: the bash guard reads the
protected branch from it, so letting the agent edit it would be a guard bypass.
It is operator-edit-only, like settings.json and the hooks themselves.
The sandbox block below is the OS-enforced boundary the guard hooks only
point at (they are a blocklist; this is deny-by-default). enabled+denyRead
closes the credential-read gap: without it a sandboxed command can still
read ~/.aws/~/.ssh. autoAllowBashIfSandboxed runs sandboxed Bash without
prompting (the OS boundary makes that safe). excludedCommands run OUTSIDE
the sandbox (gh/docker are incompatible) and fall back to normal prompts.
macOS needs nothing; Linux/WSL2 needs `bubblewrap` + `socat`. With
failIfUnavailable unset (default false), a host missing those deps WARNS and
runs unsandboxed rather than refusing to start — so shipping enabled is safe;
install the deps to actually get the boundary. The established profile sets
failIfUnavailable:true to refuse that fallback. Per profile + the
unattended-boundary rule: see docs/sandboxing.md.
